title: Hackers quickly prove that Neo Geo Doom ports are not "impossible"
gist: Within a month of a YouTuber declaring Doom technically impossible to run on the sprite-based, frame-buffer-less Neo Geo console, independent hackers produced two working (if graphically compromised) proof-of-concept ports — Doom64KB and Doom-NG — demonstrating novel software techniques to fake a frame buffer on 1990s console hardware. This is a niche retro-computing hobbyist story with no direct investable signal.

insights.claim: Two independent hacker projects, Doom64KB (by coder FrenkelS, adapting an earlier 8088/286 PC Doom port) and Doom-NG (using a VSlice renderer that steps through Doom's BSP tree), achieved functioning if graphically limited Doom ports on stock Neo Geo hardware within about a month of Modern Vintage Gamer declaring the feat technically impossible due to the console's sprite-based display and lack of a frame buffer.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: No direct investment signal; illustrative of how quickly distributed hobbyist/open-source engineering communities can falsify 'impossible' technical claims about legacy or constrained hardware once a specific challenge is publicized, a pattern occasionally relevant to open-source security/reverse-engineering research culture broadly.
insights.locator: It looks like Doom if you squint

insights.claim: Both Neo Geo Doom ports remain early and unrefined, lacking enemy logic, music, and weapon selection, and both required significant graphical compromises (Doom64KB uses 8x8 or 4x4-pixel tile blocks at very low effective resolution; Doom-NG uses a stripey column-based renderer) that would have made them unviable as marketable 1990s console products.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: No investable signal; purely a retro-gaming hobbyist technical achievement.
insights.locator: Both of these projects are still quite early
